publications.finalText: Postnobody wanted him, so i took the kid in and proved them wrong in June last year, a mom asks if i know anyone who can tutor Kevin i ask around everyone is eager until i tell them he has autism suddenly, the enthusiasm stops the communication dries up their "capacity" gets filled up overnight and nobody can tutor Kevin anymore as a tutor, i understand why tutors are like lawyers their reputation is important if they take someone in and can't deliver results words will spread and their name will be tainted "that's so unfair!", i thought to myself this is the classic case of "the rich gets richer" and "the poor gets poorer" I CANNOT let that injustice slide people shouldn't be treated like THAT so i take him in we start the grind in July, summer break while every other kid is going to Disneyland we're LOCKED IN the first test, he gets 87% then 89% then 90% by the time we are reviewing for finals, he memorized the whole textbook the result? 99% on his final exam that's 53.5/54 he goes from considering workplace math into "yeah precalc is the obvious path" GIVE PEOPLE CHANCES i know this because someone gave ME a chance too my boss took a shot on me when others wouldn't now i'm his founding engineer you might be surprised what happens Ave Christus Rex19 · 2 comments

publications.finalText: Home My Network Jobs Messaging 1 1 new notification Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,743 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 6d • 6 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn jensen huang said he'd be "deeply alarmed" if a $500k engineer only spent $5k on AI tokens i thought that was peak CEO propaganda then i did a hackathon ran codex all night had a few other things going in parallel and checked my dashboard after 77,862,001 tokens in one day rough math: $250k a year is about $685 a day at roughly $10 / 1M input and $30 / 1M output that's around 67M tokens a day so yeah the quote still sounds insane but it does NOT sound impossible anymore most people still use AI like a chatbot ask one question get one answer move on that's not what AI-native engineering looks like 1. you're running agents in parallel not one prompt multiple threads, real repos, real tasks, real context it adds up 2. you're burning tokens on actual engineering work debugging, refactors, tests, dead ends, retries, verification if you are refusing to burn tokens, you are refusing to ship 3. the bottleneck is no longer just code it's whether YOU can scope problems well enough to keep the system moving the implementation details have largely been abstracted away 4. serious reps create serious volume not because waste is good because mastery comes from running the loop again and again and again the point is not "spend more money" the point is this: if you're still treating AI like prettier Stack Overflow you're ngmi if you're building like this too, connect with me Ave Christus Rex

publications.finalText: With my previous startup experience, I can wholeheartedly agree with you that shit changes every week, and what you were prioritizing last week completely changes this week. One piece of advice that one of the co-founders I work with now told me is that you are making the best educated decision you can make at the time. You can't really be hard on yourself if that priority or whatever you were working on changes, because you made the most educated guess. No one else can make that guess for you, and only you, so it's just more about being confident in yourself that you're working on the right thing at the right time. …more

Home My Network Jobs Messaging 1 1 new notification Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,743 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 3mo • Edited • 3 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn I ran into Elon Musk last night and here’s his advices about breaking into tech: 1. Learning > Speed Shipping fast means nothing if you never understand fundamentally how things work. Spending 5 hours writing code by hand and actually understand every thing it does will give you 3-5x more benefit than telling cursor to do it in 15 minutes. If you’re just starting out on a new concept of programming, use AI to give you step by step instructions, rather than writing the code for you. After a few times of doing it manually, you now have the knowledge of how things should flow, and THIS is when you tell AI what to do. This way, you will: - Understand the system better - Write better instructions - Be able to detect bs early Funny how all of which leads to shipping faster. Sacrificing speed at first will give you 10-100x more speed later. The learning curve is real, but once you get past it, you’re golden. The point is to embrace the learning, not to cut it short. Your investment WILL pay off. 2. Just do it The truth is, nobody cares. Work on that project Make that post People care less about you than you could ever think Nobody cares if it’s cringe, or bad, or any other excuse that your mind is making up to stay in the comfort zone. Just. Do. It (for legal reasons, the photo is fake lol) Ave Christus Rex

i've made some good hires some okay hires and some misses so i've had to think pretty hard about what actually predicts whether someone can build i'm not saying leetcode is useless i'm saying people confuse interview prep with engineering skill those are NOT the same thing if i want to know whether someone can actually build here's what i care about more 1. project depth i ask what they built why they chose that architecture what broke what tradeoffs they made what they would change now 2. take-home with ambiguity i'd rather give someone a take-home where AI CAN help but one prompt can't finish the job that tells me way more i get to see judgment iteration debugging and whether they can think when the path stops being obvious 3. proof of ownership i'm trying to figure out did you actually build this or were you just nearby while it got built that's the real question traditional leetcode interviews are broken because they over-index on recall under pressure real startup engineering is messier than that it's context tradeoffs debugging communication and shipping when the spec is still blurry leetcode is mostly for interview games building is a DIFFERENT game if you're hiring engineers in 2026 what do you test for now? Ave Christus Rex

years at least i'm saying this as the founding engineer at my startup i run the technical interviews and the behavioral interviews too i've made some good hires some okay hires and some misses so i've iteration debugging and whether they can think when the path stops being obvious 3. proof of ownership i'm trying to figure out did you actually build this or were you just nearby while it got built that's the real question traditional leetcode interviews are broken because they over-index on recall under pressure real startup engineering is messier than that it's context tradeoffs debugging communication and shipping when the spec is still blurry leetcode is mostly for interview games building is a DIFFERENT game if you're hiring engineers in 2026 what do you test for now? Learning > Speed Shipping fast means nothing if you never understand fundamentally how things work. Spending 5 hours writing code by hand and actually understand every thing it does will give you 3-5x more benefit than telling cursor to do it in 15 minutes. If you’re just starting out on a new concept of programming, use AI to give you step by step instructions, rather than writing the code for you. After a few times of doing it manually, you now have the knowledge of how things should flow, and THIS is when you tell AI what to do. This way, you will: - Understand the system better - Write better instructions - Be able to detect bs early Funny how all of which leads to shipping faster. With my previous startup experience, I can wholeheartedly agree with you that shit changes every week, and what you were prioritizing last week completely changes this week. One piece of advice that one of the co-founders I work with now told me is that you are making the best educated decision you can make at the time. You can't really be hard on yourself if that priority or whatever you were working on changes, because you made the most educated guess. manualEvidence.extractedText: Home My Network Jobs Messaging 1 1 new notification Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,736 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 6d • 6 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn jensen huang said he'd be "deeply alarmed" if a $500k engineer only spent $5k on AI tokens i thought that was peak CEO propaganda then i did a hackathon ran codex all night had a few other things going in parallel and checked my dashboard after 77,862,001 tokens in one day rough math: $250k a year is about $685 a day at roughly $10 / 1M input and $30 / 1M output that's around 67M tokens a day so yeah the quote still sounds insane but it does NOT sound impossible anymore most people still use AI like a chatbot ask one question get one answer move on that's not what AI-native engineering looks like 1. you're running agents in parallel not one prompt multiple threads, real repos, real tasks, real context it adds up 2. you're burning tokens on actual engineering work debugging, refactors, tests, dead ends, retries, verification if you are refusing to burn tokens, you are refusing to ship 3. the bottleneck is no longer just code it's whether YOU can scope problems well enough to keep the system moving the implementation details have largely been abstracted away 4. serious reps create serious volume not because waste is good because mastery comes from running the loop again and again and again the point is not "spend more money" the point is this: if you're still treating AI like prettier Stack Overflow you're ngmi if you're building like this too, connect with me Ave Christus Rex

manualEvidence.extractedText: Home My Network Jobs Messaging 1 1 new notification Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,736 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 3mo • Edited • 3 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn I ran into Elon Musk last night and here’s his advices about breaking into tech: 1. Learning > Speed Shipping fast means nothing if you never understand fundamentally how things work. Spending 5 hours writing code by hand and actually understand every thing it does will give you 3-5x more benefit than telling cursor to do it in 15 minutes. If you’re just starting out on a new concept of programming, use AI to give you step by step instructions, rather than writing the code for you. After a few times of doing it manually, you now have the knowledge of how things should flow, and THIS is when you tell AI what to do. This way, you will: - Understand the system better - Write better instructions - Be able to detect bs early Funny how all of which leads to shipping faster. Sacrificing speed at first will give you 10-100x more speed later. The learning curve is real, but once you get past it, you’re golden. The point is to embrace the learning, not to cut it short. Your investment WILL pay off. 2. Just do it The truth is, nobody cares. Work on that project Make that post People care less about you than you could ever think Nobody cares if it’s cringe, or bad, or any other excuse that your mind is making up to stay in the comfort zone. Just. Do. It (for legal reasons, the photo is fake lol) Ave Christus Rex

manualEvidence.extractedText: Home My Network Jobs Messaging Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,734 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 3mo • Edited • 3 months ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn I ran into Elon Musk last night and here’s his advices about breaking into tech: 1. Learning > Speed Shipping fast means nothing if you never understand fundamentally how things work. Spending 5 hours writing code by hand and actually understand every thing it does will give you 3-5x more benefit than telling cursor to do it in 15 minutes. If you’re just starting out on a new concept of programming, use AI to give you step by step instructions, rather than writing the code for you. After a few times of doing it manually, you now have the knowledge of how things should flow, and THIS is when you tell AI what to do. This way, you will: - Understand the system better - Write better instructions - Be able to detect bs early Funny how all of which leads to shipping faster. Sacrificing speed at first will give you 10-100x more speed later. The learning curve is real, but once you get past it, you’re golden. The point is to embrace the learning, not to cut it short. Your investment WILL pay off. 2. Just do it The truth is, nobody cares. Work on that project Make that post People care less about you than you could ever think Nobody cares if it’s cringe, or bad, or any other excuse that your mind is making up to stay in the comfort zone. Just. Do. It (for legal reasons, the photo is fake lol) Ave Christus Rex

manualEvidence.extractedText: Home My Network Jobs Messaging Notifications Me For Business Post a job Edward Tran Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Surrey, British Columbia TinyFish Get 11x more profile views on average Try Premium for 50% off Profile viewers 897 Post impressions 4,733 Feed detail update Feed post Edward Tran Edward Tran • You Verified • You Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC Growth + SWE @TinyFish (47M Series A) | SWE @BLI | Ex SWE @DC 6d • 6 days ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn jensen huang said he'd be "deeply alarmed" if a $500k engineer only spent $5k on AI tokens i thought that was peak CEO propaganda then i did a hackathon ran codex all night had a few other things going in parallel and checked my dashboard after 77,862,001 tokens in one day rough math: $250k a year is about $685 a day at roughly $10 / 1M input and $30 / 1M output that's around 67M tokens a day so yeah the quote still sounds insane but it does NOT sound impossible anymore most people still use AI like a chatbot ask one question get one answer move on that's not what AI-native engineering looks like 1. you're running agents in parallel not one prompt multiple threads, real repos, real tasks, real context it adds up 2. you're burning tokens on actual engineering work debugging, refactors, tests, dead ends, retries, verification if you are refusing to burn tokens, you are refusing to ship 3. the bottleneck is no longer just code it's whether YOU can scope problems well enough to keep the system moving the implementation details have largely been abstracted away 4. serious reps create serious volume not because waste is good because mastery comes from running the loop again and again and again the point is not "spend more money" the point is this: if you're still treating AI like prettier Stack Overflow you're ngmi if you're building like this too, connect with me Ave Christus Rex

With my previous startup experience, I can wholeheartedly agree with you that shit changes every week, and what you were prioritizing last week completely changes this week. One piece of advice that one of the co-founders I work with now told me is that you are making the best educated decision you can make at the time. You can't really be hard on yourself if that priority or whatever you were working on changes, because you made the most educated guess. No one else can make that guess for you, and only you, so it's just more about being confident in yourself that you're working on the right thing at the right time. …more

big tech interview for the next 2 years at least i'm saying this as the founding engineer at my startup i run the technical interviews and the behavioral interviews too i've made some good hires some okay hires and some misses so i've had to think pretty hard about what actually predicts whether someone can build i'm not saying leetcode is useless i'm saying people confuse interview prep with engineering skill those are NOT the same thing if i want to know whether someone can actually build here's what i care about more 1. project depth i ask what they built why they chose that architecture what broke what tradeoffs they made what they would change now 2. take-home with ambiguity i'd rather give someone a take-home where AI CAN help but one prompt can't finish the job that were you just nearby while it got built that's the real question traditional leetcode interviews are broken because they over-index on recall under pressure real startup engineering is messier than that it's context tradeoffs debugging communication and shipping when the spec is still blurry leetcode is mostly for interview games building is a DIFFERENT game if you're hiring engineers in 2026 what do you test for now? Ave Christus Rex
This is such a great point about the difference between interview skills and actual engineering ability. I've found that asking candidates to walk through a past project and discuss their thought process, including the inevitable challenges and pivots, reveals so much more about their problem-solving approach and resilience than a timed coding challenge ever could 🤔. …more

manualEvidence.extractedText: With my previous startup experience, I can wholeheartedly agree with you that shit changes every week, and what you were prioritizing last week completely changes this week. One piece of advice that one of the co-founders I work with now told me is that you are making the best educated decision you can make at the time. You can't really be hard on yourself if that priority or whatever you were working on changes, because you made the most educated guess. No one else can make that guess for you, and only you, so it's just more about being confident in yourself that you're working on the right thing at the right time. …more
